title: Falafel
subtitle: Fried chickpea patties with herbs and spices, crunchy outside, tender within.
excerpt: Soaked dried chickpeas ground with onion, herbs and spices, formed into patties and fried until crisp and deep-green inside. Use dried chickpeas that have been soaked overnight; tinned chickpeas produce a dense, pasty result. Makes about 20.
sourceNotes: Falafel. The dish is associated with Egypt and the Levant; debates about its origin span Egypt, Israel and Lebanon. The version made with dried chickpeas is the most common form outside Egypt (where fava beans are traditional). Claudia Roden's A Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968) contains foundational versions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze uncooked falafel on a tray, then transfer to a bag. Fry from frozen, adding 2 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a large batch and freeze uncooked.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The falafel mixture refrigerates for up to 24 hours before cooking.

content.text: Tinned chickpeas are already cooked and have too much moisture; the falafel will be dense and will fall apart in the oil. Dried chickpeas soaked overnight are raw and starchy; the starch is what binds the patty and makes the inside light and green. This is the one recipe where substituting tinned for dried makes a completely different dish.

items.prepNote: soaked overnight in cold water; do NOT cook
thinned with lemon and water, to serve

content.text: Drain the soaked {{chickpeas-dried}} of chickpeas. Pulse in a food processor with {{onion}} onion, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{parsley-flat}} of parsley, {{coriander}} of coriander, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Process until the mixture resembles coarse wet sand; it should hold together when pressed but not be a paste. Transfer to a bowl and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Just before frying, stir {{baking-powder}} of baking powder into the mixture. With wet hands, form into patties about 4 cm across and 2 cm thick. Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a deep heavy pot to 175°C. Fry in batches of 4 to 5 for 3 to 4 minutes, turning once, until deep brown. Drain on kitchen paper. The interior should be bright green.

items.symptom: Falafel falling apart in the oil
items.cause: Tinned chickpeas used instead of soaked dried, or mixture too wet.
items.fix: Use dried soaked chickpeas. If the mixture is wet, refrigerate for an extra hour. A tablespoon of plain flour stirred in can help bind.

items.cause: Mixture over-processed to a smooth paste.
items.fix: Process to a coarse sand texture; chunks of chickpea should be visible. The air in the mixture creates lightness when it hits the hot oil.
Use a thermometer; oil at 175°C gives a quick crust that prevents the falafel absorbing oil. Below this temperature, the falafel soaks before it seals.

content.text: Falafel is one of the most widely eaten street foods in the world, sold from carts and windows across the Middle East, North Africa and everywhere the diaspora has carried it. Eaten in a pitta with tahini, pickled turnip and salad, it is a complete and satisfying meal. Made at home, freshly fried, it is a better thing than almost any version you will buy.
